Title: Positively Izzy
Author: Terri Libenson
Comment: Partly graphic novel

Title: Dog Man: Mothering Heights
Author: Dav Pilkey
Comment: Contains Lessons

Title: All the Water in the World
Author: Eiren Caffall
Comment: This was beautifully rendered story about human resilience in the face of a water covered world told from the perspective of a child who can sense water. Really interesting speculations about global warming and the breakdown in society.

Title: Natural Beauty
Author: Ling Ling Huang
Genre: Unsettling rich people doing weird things/light sci fi

Title: The Curious Charms of Arthur Pepper
Author: Phaedra Patrick
Comment: A very charming coming of late-age story confirming we never truly know the ones we love, but what an adventure we might have in the discovery.

Title: The Heaven & Earth Grocery Store
Author: James McBride
Comment: Great writing. Words make pretty

Title: How Sondheim Can Change Your Life
Author: Richard Schoch
Comment: Poignant commentary on the universal truths crafted by the greatest lyricist of our time. It’s a little inside baseball so I wouldn’t recommend this in less you were familiar with Sondheim’s body of work.

Title: You Don't Look Your Age: And Other Fairy Tales
Author: Sheila Nevins
Comment: If it had not been for an all-star voice cast and BookIt! I would have jumped ship. This series of essays by the acclaimed documentarian Sheila Nevins made me longing to know how these sometimes astute observations might have sounded in Norah Ephron’s voice. Admittedly I am not as old as the intended audience, but I am also decidedly not a second wave feminist either, and I think that’s where the real rub comes in. As a side annoyance, Nevins seems trap by some glaring body positivity issues that are hard to get past in nearly every essay, whether germane to the subject or not.

Title: Stick Dog
Author: Tom Watson
Comment: “You’ll get hungry if you read it”

Title: The Blonde Identity
Author: Ally Carter
Comment: Was this the perfect kick off the summer roadtrip book? Yes, yes it was! This is really more of a 3.5 star read for me and more like a 4.5 within the genre. This book has everything: spies, amnesia, mistaken twin identities, Europe in snowfall, and a Viking River Cruise! The dialogue is frothy without being precious and the plot is a romp of a good time.  Don’t sleep on this one, t’was a delight.

Title: A Lady's Guide to Petticoats and Piracy
Author: Mackenzi Lee

Title: Food for Thought: Essays and Ruminations
Author: Alton Brown
Comment: While these essays reach far beyond the food world, without some prior exposure to the polymath that is Alton Brown, this collection may feel a little inside baseball. I enjoyed it immensely. I even find myself watching old episodes of Iron Chef (OG, not America) and Good Eats, both blessedly streaming, based entirely on the nostalgia of this fun read!

Title: The Ballad of Songbirds and Snakes
Author: Suzanne Collins

Title: Bunny
Author: Mona Awad
Genre: Light horror, crazy on the best way

Title: The Winter Soldier
Author: Daniel Mason
Comment: Doesn’t this sound like a good book for a middle aged dad?

Title: The First Bad Man
Author: Miranda July
Genre: Unhinged but lovely middle-aged lady fiction

Title: I’m a Fan
Author: Sheena Patel
Genre: Very pretentious but sometimes interesting lady ramblings

Title: A Better Man
Author: Louise Penny
Comment: This is book 15 in the Chief Inspector Armand Gamache series. I wouldn’t recommend jumping in here, and admittedly this was probably in my bottom two of the series, but it’s worth the investment if you haven’t already heard me wax poetic about these books.

Title: Drinking Games
Author: Sarah Levy
Comment: This IS a book about someone’s sobriety journey. This is NOT from actress Sarah Levy from Schitt’s Creek as the non-Shawtie recommender suggested to me 😜

Title: Pomona Afton Can So Solve a Murder
Author: Bellamy Rose

Title: The Joy of X
Author: Steven Strogatz
Comment:
“Popular Math” as if there’s any other kind.

I want to try to cut a bagel into a Mobius strip now

Title: The Kamogawa Food Detectives
Author: Hisashi Kashiwai, Jesse Kirkwood (Translator)
Comment: This was a cozy little book in the tradition of Before the Coffee Gets Cold and We’ll Prescribe you a Cat, all with hints of magical realism. This particular book caught me because of the lush food descriptions but the plot itself was a bit thin and the magical elements never actually addressed. Good but not great, it was a cozy diverting read on a road trip.

Title: How to Sell a Haunted House
Author: Grady Hendrix

Title: Slow Horses
Author: Mick Herron
Comment: I would might rated it higher, but I already saw the TV show and knew what was coming

Title: Stone Yard Devotional
Author: Charlotte Wood
Comment: Wood delivers a story reminiscent of Elizabeth Strout, nearly entirely character driven with a plot meandering in a similar form to her subject, grief. An interesting look at the power of forgiveness and healing after loss when a woman abandons her former life to take clear and final refuge in a convent in her hometown. The not quite fixed state of memory is in juxtaposition with the rigid and reliable inner workings of the abbey until a plague of mice, a visitor from the past, and the overwhelming sense of loss changes the sureness of either setting.

Title: Full Speed to a Crash Landing
Author: Beth Revis

Title: How High We Go in the Dark
Author: Sequoia Nagamatsu
Comment: Exactly 3.5. almost DNFed in the first half but second half was a bit better

Title: The Modern Fairies
Author: Clare Pollard
Comment: A dazzlingly novel set in the famous salon of Madame D’Aulnoy with the backdrop of Versailles, a tale is woven of modern day fairytales through illusions to the Sun King’s court. Narrated with a dry wit, I found myself often laughing out loud at the intrigue, sexual exploits, and gossip that fueled Louis XIV’s reign. I found this novel enchanting…as all good fairytales are.

Title: Vera Wong’s Guide to Snooping (on a dead man)
Author: Jesse Q. Sutano

Title: The Buffalo Hunter Hunter
Author: Stephen Graham Jones
Genre: Horror with Beautiful Story and Prose

Title: Nonprofit AI
Author: Nathan Chappell and Scott Rosenkrans
Comment: Want to read a book written by AI about AI. Yeah, me neither. But I did.

Title: My Heart is a Chainsaw
Author: Stephen Graham Jones
Genre: Badass troubled young woman dealing with current and generational horrors

Title: Atmosphere
Author: Taylor Jenkins Reid
Comment: This book is right in my wheelhouse. It’s got lady astronauts and it’s set in the 80s. What more could you ask for? This is easily TJR’s best work, she generally has a penchant for writing deeply unlikable characters, but this crew won me over. It’s extremely cinematic and I won’t be the least bit surprised if this is already optioned in Hollywood. Fun summer read, more like a 3.5 star for me. I split it between kindle and audio, audio has duel narrators.
